metadata.scope_note: Seed library for psychiatry note understanding, abbreviation expansion, MSE recognition, risk-language review, and safer documentation workflows.
important_constraints: Do not reproduce DSM text verbatim.
Use authoritative sources first when possible.
Clearly separate authoritative terminology, common chart language, abbreviation mapping, ambiguity warnings, and product design inference.
If variable, local, outdated, or ambiguous, say so plainly.
source_hierarchy: Authoritative/public clinical organizations and government sources (APA, NIMH, VA/DoD, SAMHSA, CDC, NIA, Joint Commission, C-SSRS)
OpenNotes / AMA / JMIR documentation quality literature
Product-design inference from common U.S. psychiatric charting conventions requiring local validation

sources.title: Mental Status Examination - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
sources.notes: Broad MSE domains; thought, perception, cognition, insight, judgment.

sources.title: The Mental Status Examination - Clinical Methods - NCBI Bookshelf
sources.notes: Classic structured MSE description; subjective vs observational layers.

sources.title: Mental Status Examination - American Family Physician
sources.notes: Primary care framing of MSE; mood vs affect; RTIS as inferential.

sources.title: MSE Definitions - ADMSEP
sources.notes: Teaching definitions for MSE terms.

sources.title: Hallucinations - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
sources.notes: Hallucination modalities; denial possible; observe for internal stimuli.

sources.title: Delusions - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
sources.notes: Delusion concept and types; clinical elicitation cautions.

sources.title: Mood Disorder - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
sources.notes: Mood as pervasive and sustained internal feeling tone.

sources.title: Depression - NIMH
sources.notes: Symptoms include sleep, appetite, energy, concentration.

sources.title: What Is Depression? - APA
sources.notes: Symptoms including agitation/retardation, concentration, death thoughts.

sources.title: Bipolar Disorder - NIMH
sources.notes: Mania symptoms incl decreased need for sleep, racing thoughts, impulsivity.

sources.title: Schizophrenia - NIMH
sources.notes: Psychosis symptoms, thought disorder, motivation, social withdrawal.

sources.title: Understanding Psychosis - NIMH
sources.notes: Early psychosis signs and symptom descriptions.

sources.title: TIP 57: Trauma-Informed Care in Behavioral Health Services - SAMHSA
sources.notes: Trauma definition; hypervigilance; self-harm distinction; trauma-informed language.

sources.title: PTSD and DSM-5 - PTSD: National Center for PTSD
sources.notes: PTSD symptom clusters, depersonalization, derealization.

sources.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder - NIMH
sources.notes: Social communication differences and restricted/repetitive behaviors.

sources.title: Symptoms and Diagnosis of ADHD - CDC
sources.notes: Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity symptom framing.

sources.title: What Is Dementia? - National Institute on Aging
sources.notes: Dementia as decline interfering with daily life.

sources.title: What Is Mild Cognitive Impairment? - National Institute on Aging
sources.notes: MCI as more thinking/memory problems than expected, often still independent.

sources.title: Delirium - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
sources.notes: Acute fluctuating disturbance in attention, awareness, cognition.

sources.title: Borderline Personality Disorder - NIMH
sources.notes: Emotion regulation difficulty, impulsivity, unstable self/relationships.

sources.title: What are Personality Disorders? - APA
sources.notes: Long-term patterns affecting thinking, emotion, behavior, relationships.

sources.title: Highlights of Changes from DSM-IV-TR to DSM-5 - APA
sources.notes: ASD umbrella; ADHD presentation wording; legacy terminology changes.

sources.title: What is Intellectual Disability? - APA
sources.notes: Intellectual disability replaces mental retardation.

sources.title: Resources for Suicide Risk Reduction - The Joint Commission
sources.notes: Validated screening + assessment should ask ideation, plan, intent, self-harm, risk, protective factors.

sources.title: National Performance Goals Effective January 2026 - Joint Commission
sources.notes: Screening expectations and documentation.

sources.title: Ask Suicide-Screening Questions Toolkit - NIMH
sources.notes: ASQ screening; BSSA workflows.

sources.title: Adult Outpatient Brief Suicide Safety Assessment Guide - NIMH
sources.notes: Plan, intent, access to means, safety planning, no-suicide-contract warning.

sources.title: Definitions of Suicidal Ideation and Behavior - C-SSRS
sources.notes: Ideation severity ladder incl intent and specific plan definitions.

sources.title: VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for the Assessment and Management of Patients at Risk for Suicide (2024)
sources.notes: Warning signs, acute/chronic risk, lethal means counseling.

sources.title: TIP 50: Addressing Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors in Substance Abuse Treatment - SAMHSA
sources.notes: Definitions of death ideation, NSSI, suicide attempt, preparatory behavior; avoid 'gesture'.

sources.title: Danger in Discharge Summaries: Abbreviations Create Confusion - AHRQ PSNet
sources.notes: Abbreviations in EHRs increase misunderstandings; no abbreviation understood identically by all respondents.

sources.title: The Joint Commission FAQ on Abbreviations/Acronyms
sources.notes: Organizations should standardize approved abbreviations and entries.

sources.title: Examples of Non-Stigmatizing Language and Alternatives - CDC
sources.notes: Person-first SUD language; use positive/negative toxicology rather than clean/dirty.

sources.title: Words Matter - NIDA
sources.notes: Preferred person-first language for addiction; terms to avoid.

sources.title: TIP 63: Medications for Opioid Use Disorder - SAMHSA
sources.notes: MOUD framing and approved medications.

sources.title: Open Notes for Mental Health Clinicians - OpenNotes
sources.notes: Sensitive documentation, inviting patients into note process.

sources.title: Mental Health Toolkit - OpenNotes
sources.notes: Plain-language shifts and documenting sensitive issues.

sources.title: Guidelines for Patient-Centered Documentation in the Era of Open Notes - JMIR Med Educ 2025
sources.notes: Guidelines include respectful language, avoiding acronyms and judgment.

sources.title: How Should Clinicians Respond When Patients Read Pejorative Language in Their Health Records? - AMA Journal of Ethics
sources.notes: Pejorative language, person-first framing.

sources.title: Copy and Paste - AHRQ PSNet
sources.notes: Inaccurate copied/autopopulated text can be unsafe and unethical.

sources.title: Medical Records Often Include Stigmatizing Language, and AI Can Help - APA News Release
sources.notes: Stigmatizing note language matters and is detectable.

lexicon.definition: Persistent low or sad mood reported by the patient.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Usually means sustained sadness, emptiness, or low mood rather than a brief bad day.
lexicon.plain_language: feeling down most of the time

lexicon.definition: Unpleasant distressed mood state that can include sadness, irritability, or unease.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often used when the patient appears distressed and the exact valence is mixed.

lexicon.definition: Mood within a typical, not clearly depressed or manic range.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Used to document no clear pathologic mood syndrome at the time of interview.

lexicon.definition: Mood marked by low frustration tolerance and easy annoyance.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: May indicate depression, mania, trauma-related arousal, withdrawal, pain, or personality traits.

lexicon.definition: Subjective state of worry, tension, or fear.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Commonly documents distress, anticipatory worry, and physiologic unease.

lexicon.definition: Overly elevated, overly confident, or unusually uninhibited mood.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often charted in mania when affect and interpersonal style are overly broad or grandiose.

lexicon.definition: Markedly elevated happy mood out of proportion to context.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often used in mania or hypomania rather than simple good mood.

lexicon.definition: Rapid shifts in reported emotional state.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Used when the patient describes fast mood changes during the encounter or recently.

lexicon.definition: Diminished interest, initiative, or emotional engagement.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often used in neurocognitive or psychotic presentations when motivation is reduced.

lexicon.definition: Reduced ability to enjoy normally pleasurable activities.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Commonly documented in depression and some psychotic disorders.

lexicon.definition: Sense that the future will not improve or that nothing can help.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Important because it often strengthens clinical concern in depression and suicide assessment.
lexicon.plain_language: feels like nothing will get better

lexicon.definition: Belief or feeling of low worth or excessive self-blame.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common depressive cognitions with risk significance when severe.

lexicon.definition: Observable emotional expression shows a broad expected range.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Usually means affect is not notably restricted.
lexicon.plain_language: shows a normal range of emotion

lexicon.definition: Wide range of observable emotional expression.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often used interchangeably with full range, though some clinicians mean especially expressive.

lexicon.definition: Noticeably narrowed range of observed emotion.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: The patient shows some emotion, but less variety than expected.

lexicon.definition: Reduced observable range of emotional expression.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often synonymous with constricted; local preference varies.
lexicon.notes: Often local/preference-based rather than sharply distinct.

lexicon.definition: Substantially decreased intensity of emotional expression.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Used when emotion is present but clearly muted.

lexicon.definition: Near absence of observable emotional expression.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Used for markedly reduced facial, vocal, and gestural expression.
lexicon.notes: High overstatement risk; should reflect clear observation.

lexicon.definition: Rapid shifts in observable emotional expression.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Affect changes quickly during interview.
lexicon.plain_language: emotions shift quickly on the outside

lexicon.definition: Observed affect matches the reported mood or discussed content.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Used when outward expression fits the emotional topic.
lexicon.plain_language: looks how they say they feel

lexicon.definition: Observed affect does not match reported mood or topic.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Clinically important in psychosis, mania, trauma, and some neurocognitive states.
lexicon.plain_language: expression doesn't fit what is being discussed

lexicon.definition: Observable crying or near-crying emotional expression.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often documents visible sadness or overwhelm without making a stronger syndrome claim.

lexicon.definition: Ideas proceed in a direct, coherent sequence.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often shorthand for understandable and organized thinking.
lexicon.plain_language: thoughts make sense step by step

lexicon.definition: Thoughts connect in a manner that is internally understandable.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Usually paired with linear or goal-directed in routine notes.

lexicon.definition: Speech stays organized around answering the question or reaching a point.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often used to distinguish organized thought from tangential or circumstantial speech.

lexicon.definition: Overinclusive or overly detailed speech that eventually returns to the point.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Important to distinguish from tangential speech, which does not return.
lexicon.plain_language: too much detail but gets there eventually

lexicon.definition: Speech departs from the topic and does not return to answer the question.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often indicates more significant disorganization than circumstantiality.
lexicon.plain_language: goes off track and never comes back

lexicon.definition: Rapid shifts from idea to idea with understandable but accelerated associations.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Classic manic language pattern; may sound pressured and hard to interrupt.

lexicon.definition: Connections between ideas are weak or difficult to follow.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: More severe thought disorganization than tangentiality.

lexicon.definition: Sudden interruption in the train of thought.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can appear in psychosis, anxiety, trauma, or cognitive disorder; do not overinfer cause.

lexicon.definition: Persistent repetition of the same idea, topic, or response.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: May reflect anxiety, OCD, neurocognitive disorder, autism, or frontal dysfunction.
lexicon.plain_language: keeps returning to the same thing

lexicon.definition: Repetitive, hard-to-disengage thinking, often around distressing themes.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common in depression, anxiety, trauma, and some personality presentations.

lexicon.definition: Limited abstract reasoning; interprets concepts literally.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Relevant in neurodevelopmental, neurocognitive, psychotic, and some baseline cognitive states.

lexicon.definition: Thought form is sufficiently disordered that organization is difficult to follow overall.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Broad term best used when more specific descriptors are unavailable or multiple abnormalities are present.

lexicon.definition: Fixed false belief held despite strong contrary evidence and not simply explained by shared culture or religion.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Use only when the record supports conviction, fixity, and non-correction.
lexicon.notes: High-certainty term; avoid if evidence only suggests suspicion or unusual belief.

lexicon.definition: Fixed belief that one is being harmed, followed, conspired against, or targeted.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common psychosis documentation subtype.
lexicon.plain_language: belief others are out to get them

lexicon.definition: Fixed exaggerated belief about power, identity, wealth, or ability.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often linked to mania or psychosis.
lexicon.plain_language: belief of special powers or status

lexicon.definition: Belief that neutral events or communications specifically refer to oneself.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can be less fixed than a full delusion and is often charted as possible psychotic content.
lexicon.plain_language: thinks ordinary things are about them

lexicon.definition: Recurrent intrusive thought, image, or urge experienced as difficult to dismiss.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Usually ego-dystonic and anxiety-provoking in OCD-spectrum contexts.

lexicon.definition: Prominent repeated focus on a topic or concern without necessarily being delusional or obsessive.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Useful when content is dominant but not clearly pathognomonic.

lexicon.definition: Repeated focus on distressing themes or perceived failures.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common in depression and anxiety notes.

lexicon.definition: Thought content centered on disproportionate fear of a specific trigger or situation.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Use if phobic focus is clearly documented.

lexicon.definition: Disproportionate self-blame or guilt-laden cognitions.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Relevant in depression and sometimes psychotic guilt themes.

lexicon.definition: Thoughts about death, wanting to die, or taking one’s own life; severity varies.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Must be captured with severity and context rather than as a binary alone.

lexicon.definition: Thoughts about killing or seriously harming another person.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Requires context, target, plan, intent, and access details when present.
lexicon.plain_language: thoughts of harming or killing someone else

lexicon.definition: Perception-like experience occurring without an external stimulus.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Modality should be specified whenever possible.

lexicon.definition: Hearing voices, sounds, music, or other auditory phenomena without an external source.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Should document whether voices are conversational, commentary, command, internal/external sounding, and distressing.

lexicon.definition: Auditory hallucinations instructing the person to perform an act.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Particularly important for risk review; content and intent matter.
lexicon.plain_language: voices telling them to do things

lexicon.definition: Seeing people, objects, lights, or scenes without an external source.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often prompts broader medical and neurologic differential, not only primary psychosis.

lexicon.definition: False sensation of touch or crawling/tingling without stimulus.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can occur with intoxication, withdrawal, neurologic conditions, or psychosis.
lexicon.plain_language: feeling things on the skin when nothing is there

lexicon.definition: Misinterpretation of a real external stimulus.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Do not auto-convert to hallucination.
lexicon.plain_language: mistaking something real for something else

lexicon.definition: Experience of feeling detached from oneself or as if observing oneself from outside.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can occur in trauma, panic, dissociation, and other conditions.
lexicon.plain_language: feels unreal or detached from self

lexicon.definition: Experience that the external world feels unreal, distant, or dreamlike.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can occur in trauma, panic, dissociation, and other conditions.

lexicon.definition: Observed behavior suggesting attention to experiences not shared by the examiner, such as pausing, looking around, or talking as if to unseen others.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Inferential observation, not a direct synonym for hallucinations.

lexicon.definition: Awake and responsive to the environment.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Basic arousal descriptor, not a full cognitive assessment.

lexicon.definition: Correctly oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Shorthand varies locally between x3 and x4.
lexicon.notes: Local shorthand; some teams use x3 only.

lexicon.definition: Difficulty sustaining, focusing, or shifting attention.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can reflect delirium, ADHD, anxiety, mania, psychosis, intoxication, or fatigue.

lexicon.definition: Impairment in encoding, retaining, or retrieving information.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Specify immediate, recent, or remote when possible.

lexicon.definition: Difficulty planning, organizing, sequencing, or adapting behavior.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often inferred from testing or functional description rather than casual interview alone.

lexicon.definition: Acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention, awareness, and cognition that develops over a short period.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Medical emergency framing is often appropriate; quiet or hypoactive forms can be missed.
lexicon.plain_language: sudden confusion that comes and goes

lexicon.definition: Progressive cognitive decline that interferes with everyday function.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Clinical notes may still say dementia; formal taxonomy may say major neurocognitive disorder.

lexicon.definition: Cognitive decline greater than expected for age but not clearly preventing independence.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Important not to up-code as dementia without support.

lexicon.definition: Patient shows understanding of symptoms, condition, or need for treatment.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often subjective and should reflect actual evidence from the interview.

lexicon.definition: Patient shows partial but incomplete awareness of symptoms or need for care.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common but vague rating.

lexicon.definition: Limited awareness or acknowledgment of illness, symptoms, or consequences.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Should be supported by examples rather than asserted as a label alone.

lexicon.definition: Difficulty making safe or sound decisions in context.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Best when supported by examples such as unsafe actions, poor planning, or inability to appreciate consequences.

lexicon.definition: Engages with interview and follows conversational structure.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common, low-risk observational descriptor.

lexicon.definition: Reserved, withholding, or cautious in engagement with the examiner.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can mean defensive, suspicious, minimally disclosing, or simply private; local use varies.

lexicon.definition: Increased movement or inability to sit still.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Could reflect anxiety, agitation, akathisia, mania, stimulant use, or pain.

lexicon.definition: Observable increase in purposeless or semi-purposeful motor activity.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can be depressive, anxious, manic, delirious, or medication-related.
lexicon.plain_language: moving more than usual because distressed

lexicon.definition: Observable slowing of movement, speech, or initiation.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common in depression and some neurocognitive/medical states.

lexicon.definition: Reduced engagement, social contact, or spontaneous participation.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: May reflect depression, psychosis, autism, trauma, or temperament.

lexicon.definition: Poorly groomed or unkempt appearance.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Useful observable descriptor that is less inferential than 'neglecting self-care'.

lexicon.definition: Reduced or inconsistent eye contact during interview.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Not specific; can reflect culture, autism, trauma, anxiety, psychosis, or discomfort.
lexicon.plain_language: doesn't look at the interviewer much

lexicon.definition: Speech is rapid, difficult to interrupt, and driven.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Classic manic descriptor but not every fast speaker is pressured.
lexicon.plain_language: talking very fast and hard to interrupt

lexicon.definition: Noticeable delay before answering or initiating speech.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Could reflect depression, psychosis, cognitive slowing, language disorder, or guardedness.

lexicon.definition: Speech is low clarity or articulation, making understanding difficult.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Descriptive but should not imply thought disorder by itself.

lexicon.definition: Markedly reduced spontaneous speech output.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common in negative symptoms, severe depression, delirium, or neurocognitive states.

lexicon.definition: Difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, or waking earlier than desired.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Needs subtype when possible.

lexicon.definition: Difficulty falling asleep at the start of the night.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common but often omitted when only 'insomnia' is charted.

lexicon.definition: Waking during the night and struggling to resume sleep.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common depressive/anxiety/PTSD complaint.

lexicon.definition: Waking too early in the morning and being unable to return to sleep.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often considered a depressive pattern, but not specific.

lexicon.definition: Excessive sleep or excessive daytime sleepiness.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can occur in depression, meds, sleep disorders, neurocognitive disease, etc.

lexicon.definition: Reduced need for sleep without feeling tired.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: High-yield manic symptom; distinct from insomnia.
lexicon.plain_language: needs less sleep and still feels energized

lexicon.definition: Reduced desire to eat or reduced intake.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Capture whether due to mood, nausea, psychosis, stimulant use, etc. when known.

lexicon.definition: Increased desire to eat or increased intake.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often charted in depression, atypical presentations, meds, or binge eating contexts.

lexicon.definition: Reduced energy or sense of being drained.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common across depression, medical illness, sleep problems, medication effects.

lexicon.definition: Difficulty sustaining mental focus or task attention.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can reflect mood, anxiety, trauma, ADHD, psychosis, sleep deprivation, or delirium.

lexicon.definition: Exposure to an event, series of events, or circumstances experienced as harmful or life-threatening with lasting adverse effects possible.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Do not auto-convert exposure into PTSD or trauma disorder.

lexicon.definition: Unwanted recurrent memories of a traumatic event.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Important to distinguish from psychotic phenomena.

lexicon.definition: Episodes in which the person feels or acts as if the traumatic event is recurring.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Do not auto-map all vivid memories to flashbacks.

lexicon.definition: Heightened scanning for threat or exaggerated alertness to danger.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can resemble paranoia but is not the same thing.

lexicon.definition: Heightened startle response to cues or unexpected stimuli.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often paired with hypervigilance in trauma documentation.

lexicon.definition: Efforts to avoid trauma reminders, thoughts, feelings, people, or places.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Needs context; avoidance can also occur in anxiety generally.

lexicon.definition: Disruption or detachment in awareness, memory, identity, or perception.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Broad umbrella term; specify subtype when possible.

lexicon.definition: Reduced emotional responsiveness following trauma or severe distress.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can resemble depression or negative symptoms.

lexicon.definition: Showing effects of a substance on mental status, behavior, or physiology.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Substance and degree should be specified when known.

lexicon.definition: Symptoms or signs occurring after reducing or stopping a substance.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Substance should be specified; may involve psychiatric and medical risk.

lexicon.definition: Strong desire or urge to use a substance.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Useful in relapse risk and treatment planning.

lexicon.definition: Episode of heavy or concentrated substance use over a short period.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common chart phrase but threshold varies by substance and clinician.
lexicon.plain_language: used a lot at once / for a stretch

lexicon.definition: Previously met disorder criteria but currently doing better with reduced or absent symptoms/use.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Need timeframe and whether based on patient report, tox, or treatment records.

lexicon.definition: Process of improving health and wellness and living a self-directed life after substance-related problems.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often patient-centered, but not identical to abstinence.

lexicon.definition: Toxicology result indicates the tested substance or class was detected.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Use 'positive'/'negative' rather than 'dirty'/'clean'.

lexicon.definition: Toxicology result did not detect the tested substance or class.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Use 'negative' rather than 'clean'.
lexicon.plain_language: tox screen did not show substance

lexicon.definition: Medication treatment for OUD such as buprenorphine, methadone, or naltrexone.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Preferred clinical framing is MOUD rather than implying substitution or failure.

lexicon.definition: Thoughts about being dead or not waking up without a stated plan to kill oneself.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Do not soften into 'no SI' or strengthen into active SI without support.

lexicon.definition: Thoughts of killing oneself beyond a passive wish to be dead.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Needs severity details such as method, plan, intent, frequency, controllability.

lexicon.definition: Suicidal ideation with identified method or scenario for attempting suicide.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: More concerning than nonspecific ideation; access to means matters.

lexicon.definition: Expectation or desire to act on suicidal thoughts.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Intent is not the same as merely having a method in mind.

lexicon.definition: Behavior indicating movement toward a suicide attempt such as gathering means or writing a note.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Not the same as ideation alone.

lexicon.definition: Self-directed potentially injurious behavior with at least some intent to die.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Do not use for nonsuicidal self-injury or accidental overdose without evidence of intent.

lexicon.definition: Self-directed self-harm without suicidal intent.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Important to separate from suicide attempts while still taking seriously.

lexicon.definition: Umbrella term for self-harming behavior; intent may be unclear unless specified.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Do not assume suicidal or nonsuicidal intent from SIB alone.

lexicon.definition: Availability of firearms, medications, ligatures, or other means relevant to the person’s risk scenario.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Risk review should capture access separately from ideation.
lexicon.plain_language: has access to a way to act

lexicon.definition: Factors that may buffer or reduce risk, such as reasons for living, supports, and willingness to seek help.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Protective factors do not cancel risk factors; they are a separate field.
lexicon.plain_language: things that help keep the person safe

lexicon.definition: Collaboratively documented coping steps, supports, and emergency actions for future crisis periods.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Not the same as a no-suicide contract.
lexicon.plain_language: plan for what to do if things get worse

lexicon.definition: Rapid shifts in emotion, often reactive to interpersonal stressors.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can overlap with trauma, bipolar symptoms, and general emotional lability.
lexicon.plain_language: emotions shift quickly and react strongly

lexicon.definition: Tendency to act quickly without adequate forethought.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can reflect ADHD, mania, trauma, intoxication, or personality pathology.

lexicon.definition: Unstable or markedly shifting sense of self.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Most associated with borderline personality documentation but can appear elsewhere.
lexicon.plain_language: unstable sense of who one is

lexicon.definition: All-or-nothing appraisal, often alternating idealization and devaluation.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Useful only when clearly supported by relational pattern data.
lexicon.plain_language: sees people as all good or all bad

lexicon.definition: Attributing unrealistic positive qualities to another person.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Often appears in alternating relational patterns.

lexicon.definition: Markedly negative reappraisal of another person after idealization or disappointment.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Should be supported by pattern rather than one angry comment.
lexicon.plain_language: sudden drop from all-good to all-bad

lexicon.definition: Inflexible interpersonal or cognitive style that persists across contexts.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Generic descriptor; should not stand in for a diagnosis.

lexicon.definition: Pattern of distractibility, poor sustained attention, and difficulty following through.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can occur in ADHD but is not specific to ADHD.

lexicon.definition: Excessive movement or motor activity beyond the developmental context.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common ADHD descriptor but not specific to it.

lexicon.definition: Blurting, interrupting, or acting without waiting.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Overlap exists with mania, substance use, trauma, and personality traits.

lexicon.definition: Persistent difficulty with reciprocal social interaction or communication.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Avoid overpathologizing one behavior such as poor eye contact.

lexicon.definition: Highly focused or unusually intense interests.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Needs developmental and functional context.

lexicon.definition: Repetitive motor, verbal, or ritualized behaviors.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Common autism descriptor, but can also occur in OCD or tic disorders.

lexicon.definition: Heightened or unusual responses to sensory input.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Can be trauma-related or migraine-related too; context matters.

lexicon.definition: Developmental skills emerge later than expected for age.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Broad descriptive term; avoid inferring etiology from it alone.

lexicon.definition: Condition involving limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive functioning beginning during development.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Preferred term replacing mental retardation.

lexicon.definition: Neurodevelopmental condition involving persistent social communication differences and restricted or repetitive behaviors.
lexicon.common_documentation_meaning: Preferred umbrella term; older subtype labels may appear in legacy notes.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Depressive disorder abbreviation used widely in psych and primary care.
Common psychiatric abbreviation; in non-psych contexts can also mean bipolar disorder in older notes or bronchopulmonary dysplasia.
abbreviations.notes: Do not auto-expand to borderline personality disorder without psych context.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Common but ambiguous; also used for behavior disorder, brain disease, benzodiazepine dependence in local notes.

abbreviations.notes: Common in older notes; 'BAD' is visually risky in patient-facing contexts.

abbreviations.expansion: social anxiety disorder OR seasonal affective disorder

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Preferred taxonomy in developmental context, but 'ID' is highly ambiguous elsewhere.
Often used in pediatrics/psych; also differential diagnosis.
Formal taxonomy abbreviation; can be confused with noncommunicable disease in general medicine.
Common abbreviation in geriatrics and psych.
Psych abbreviation but also emergency department shorthand in some workflows.
Thoughts of suicide; severity must be further qualified.
Thoughts of killing or seriously harming others.
Self-harming behavior with intent unspecified unless otherwise documented.
Combined shorthand when both are reported.
Hearing voices or sounds without external stimulus.
Suspicious or persecutory thoughts, often below certainty of delusion.
Belief neutral stimuli refer specifically to self.
Sudden interruption in train of thought.
MSE domain shorthand for insight and judgment.

abbreviations.expansion: alert and oriented to person, place, time
alert and oriented to person, place, time, situation
abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Common cognitive shorthand with local variability.
Generic shorthand. Often too vague for psych note understanding.
Medical shorthand that may appear in psych intake templates.
Observed behavior suggesting attention to experiences not shared by examiner.
Legacy/common shorthand for sleep, appetite, energy, concentration changes.
abbreviations.notes: Common but nonstandard; define explicitly in UI if used.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Risk/legal shorthand often used in ED/inpatient settings.
Risk/legal shorthand for violence risk to others.
Legal/hold criterion shorthand in some states.
abbreviations.notes: Highly state- and facility-specific legal meaning.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Continuous observation level often used for imminent risk or severe disorganization.
Follow-up assessment process after positive screen.
Structured day program level of care.
Workflow/service shorthand common in mental health systems.
History from family/others or outside records.
Authorization/workflow for obtaining or sharing records.
Missed appointment shorthand; also saline or normal strength in other contexts.
Psychotherapy abbreviation; also assertive community treatment.
Trauma therapy abbreviation; also physical exam or physical education.
Trauma therapy abbreviation; also Current Procedural Terminology.
Common chart shorthand for alcohol use/intoxication.
Older/common term for medications for addiction/OUD treatment.
abbreviations.notes: Still common, but many organizations now prefer MOUD/MOAT depending condition.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Legacy shorthand; patient-centered alternatives are preferred.
abbreviations.notes: Use 'injection drug use' or person-centered phrasing when writing.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Medication frequency shorthand used in psychiatry and all medicine.
Medication timing shorthand; abbreviation risk exists in some safety programs.
abbreviations.notes: Often better stored as standardized frequency code, not free text expansion.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Antipsychotic and sometimes other psych medication formulation shorthand.
Very risky shorthand because NA has many meanings.
State-specific legal shorthand; not portable nationally.
abbreviations.notes: Do not normalize nationally without a state/legal layer.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: State-specific legal shorthand; not portable nationally.
abbreviations.notes: Do not normalize nationally without a state/legal layer.

abbreviations.psych_context_meaning: Discharge shorthand; can also mean American Medical Association.

mse_library.concise_definition: Appearance suggests intact hygiene and self-care at interview.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful neutral observable descriptor for appearance.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs dressed well for one encounter but broadly functionally impaired
understatement_risks: May underdocument self-neglect history if only current snapshot is clean

mse_library.concise_definition: Unkempt appearance or impaired grooming.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Descriptive appearance term with less inference than 'self-neglect'.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs casual dress or culturally normative appearance
overstatement_risks: Can be overstated from minor grooming difference
understatement_risks: May understate severe self-neglect if only 'unkempt' is used

mse_library.concise_definition: Engages appropriately in interview and follows interview structure.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs agreeable or fully endorsing treatment
overstatement_risks: Can overstate alliance if patient is superficially cooperative

mse_library.concise_definition: Reserved, withholding, or cautious style with interviewer.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs shy, avoidant, suspicious, or paranoid
overstatement_risks: Can imply pathology when the patient is simply private or mistrustful for contextual reasons
understatement_risks: May understate true suspiciousness if used instead of clearer language

mse_library.concise_definition: Increased motor activity or difficulty remaining still.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Use when movement increase is present but source unclear.
overstatement_risks: Can be mislabeled as agitation or mania
understatement_risks: Can underrecognize medication side effects

mse_library.concise_definition: Observable increased non-goal-directed or semi-purposeful movement linked to distress.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful in depression, delirium, anxiety, or mania.
understatement_risks: May undercall agitation if using only 'restless'

mse_library.concise_definition: Observable slowing of movement, initiation, or gesture.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Often used in depression or severe cognitive/medical states.
overstatement_risks: Can be confounded by sedation or physical disability
understatement_risks: May undercapture severity if only 'slowed' is used

mse_library.concise_definition: Reduced engagement or spontaneous participation.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs guarded, depressed, autistic social communication differences
understatement_risks: May understate catatonia, depression, or psychosis

mse_library.concise_definition: Behavior crosses interpersonal or conversational boundaries.
understatement_risks: May understate aggression if actual threats or intimidation occurred

mse_library.concise_definition: Speech parameters appear within expected range.
understatement_risks: May miss subtle latency, pressure, or prosody issues

mse_library.concise_definition: Rapid, hard-to-interrupt, driven speech.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: High-yield manic sign when clearly present.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs fast speech from anxiety or caffeine
overstatement_risks: Can overstate mania if the speaker is simply verbose
understatement_risks: May understate mania if only 'talkative' is used

mse_library.concise_definition: Speech rate is faster than expected.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful descriptive alternative when true pressure is uncertain.
overstatement_risks: Can still be overread as mania
understatement_risks: May understate clinically pressured speech

mse_library.concise_definition: Speech rate is slower than expected.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Often paired with depression, delirium, or sedation.
overstatement_risks: Can overread fatigue or language processing issues
understatement_risks: May understate severe psychomotor retardation

mse_library.concise_definition: Speech volume is reduced.
overstatement_risks: Can reflect hearing context rather than pathology
understatement_risks: May understate marked psychomotor slowing

mse_library.concise_definition: Long pauses before initiating responses.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs thought blocking, hearing impairment, translation delays
understatement_risks: May underdocument formal thought disruption

mse_library.concise_definition: Markedly reduced spontaneous verbal output.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Common in negative symptoms, depression, or delirium.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize delirium or catatonia

mse_library.concise_definition: Patient reports sustained low mood.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Mood is subjective and should reflect patient report.
overstatement_risks: Can overstate a syndrome from one descriptor
understatement_risks: May understate severity if passive SI/hopelessness omitted

mse_library.concise_definition: Patient reports worry, fear, tension, or nervousness.
overstatement_risks: May pathologize situational anxiety without context
understatement_risks: May undercapture panic or trauma physiology

mse_library.concise_definition: No clearly depressed or manic mood evident by report.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs neutral affect or simply 'doing okay'
overstatement_risks: Can overstate wellness if symptoms persist in other domains
understatement_risks: May obscure chronic anxiety, apathy, or mixed states

mse_library.concise_definition: Observable affect matches reported mood or discussed content.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful when distinction between mood and affect is respected.
overstatement_risks: Can be boilerplate if mood not separately documented

mse_library.concise_definition: Observable emotion does not fit reported mood or topic.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Potentially important in psychosis or severe dissociation.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs nervous laughter, cultural style, defensive humor
understatement_risks: May understate psychosis if specific examples omitted

mse_library.concise_definition: Narrowed emotional range.
understatement_risks: May underdocument more severe flattening

mse_library.concise_definition: Markedly reduced emotional intensity.
overstatement_risks: Can be overstated when person is simply stoic or tired
understatement_risks: May understate near-absence of expression

mse_library.concise_definition: Near absence of visible emotional expression.
understatement_risks: May under-recognize severe negative symptoms if avoided when clearly present

mse_library.concise_definition: Affect shifts rapidly during interview.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful when emotional expression changes quickly in ways not fully explained by topic shifts.
understatement_risks: May understate manic or neurological lability if specifics omitted

mse_library.concise_definition: Ideas proceed coherently in sequence.
overstatement_risks: Can become rote if used in every note
understatement_risks: May undercapture mild circumstantiality

mse_library.concise_definition: Speech answers questions and reaches a point.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful because it is more behaviorally grounded than 'normal'.
overstatement_risks: Can overstate organization in a structured interview
understatement_risks: May underrecognize disorganization outside interview scaffolding

mse_library.concise_definition: Overinclusive detail but eventually returns to the answer.
understatement_risks: May understate significant inefficiency if 'linear' is used instead

mse_library.concise_definition: Departs from topic and does not return.
overstatement_risks: Can be overstated when interviewer loses structure
understatement_risks: May understate thought disorder if only 'verbose' is used

mse_library.concise_definition: Weak or hard-to-follow associations between ideas.
overstatement_risks: Should not be used for merely idiosyncratic speech
understatement_risks: May understate psychosis if softened to 'disorganized' without examples

mse_library.concise_definition: Rapid associative shifts with understandable links.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Often manic when paired with pressure and decreased need for sleep.
overstatement_risks: Can be mistaken for ADHD, anxiety, or enthusiasm
understatement_risks: May understate mania if recorded only as 'rapid speech'

mse_library.concise_definition: Sudden interruption in train of thought.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize trauma/dissociation or cognitive causes

mse_library.concise_definition: Content supports a fixed false belief not corrected by evidence.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs overvalued idea, suspiciousness, trauma-based fear
understatement_risks: May undercall true delusion if clinicians avoid the term despite clear evidence

mse_library.concise_definition: Content reflects being harmed, watched, followed, or targeted.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Can be used before escalating to full delusion if certainty is incomplete.
understatement_risks: May understate psychosis if fixity and conviction are not documented

mse_library.concise_definition: Intrusive ego-dystonic thought content.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Use when repetitive thoughts are recognized as unwanted.
overstatement_risks: Can be mislabeled when content is simply repeated worry
understatement_risks: May understate OCD when compulsions are omitted

mse_library.concise_definition: Dominant topic focus without enough evidence for obsession or delusion.
understatement_risks: May understate clinically important psychosis or suicidality if specifics omitted

mse_library.concise_definition: Patient does not report hallucinations when asked.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Safer than asserting hallucinations are absent in all contexts.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs no evidence of hallucinations observed
overstatement_risks: Can overstate if patient was not directly asked
understatement_risks: May miss non-auditory modalities or reluctance to disclose

mse_library.concise_definition: Perception of sounds/voices without external source.
overstatement_risks: Can misclassify trauma intrusions or culturally normative experiences
understatement_risks: May underdocument risk if command content omitted

mse_library.concise_definition: Visual percepts without external source.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Should prompt broader differential than primary psychosis alone.
understatement_risks: May miss medical causes if reduced to 'psychosis'

mse_library.concise_definition: Misinterpretation of a real stimulus.
Observed behavior suggesting attention to experiences not shared by examiner.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Observation, not direct confirmation of hallucinations.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs self-talk, distraction, hearing aid/phone use
understatement_risks: May underdocument psychosis if used alone instead of pairing with direct report when available

mse_library.concise_definition: Experiences of self or world feeling unreal or detached.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Often trauma/panic related rather than psychotic.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize dissociation if collapsed into 'anxiety'

mse_library.concise_definition: Awake and responsive.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize delirium if attention not documented

mse_library.concise_definition: Accurately identifies person, place, time, and possibly situation.
understatement_risks: May understate major deficits if only binary orientation is charted

mse_library.concise_definition: Able to sustain and manipulate attention during exam.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Useful because delirium often impairs attention first.
understatement_risks: May undercall delirium or ADHD if not specifically assessed

mse_library.concise_definition: No clear memory deficit evident on interview.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize subtle MCI

mse_library.concise_definition: Abstract reasoning appears limited or literal.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Often inferred from proverb/similarity testing or conversational style.
important_distinctions_from_similar_term: vs low education, language difference, autism-related literal style
understatement_risks: May under-recognize genuine executive dysfunction if not explored

mse_library.concise_definition: Acute fluctuating inattention/awareness/cognition state.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Should trigger medical review, not just psych labeling.
overstatement_risks: High overstatement risk if acute course not supported
understatement_risks: High understatement risk because hypoactive delirium is often missed

mse_library.concise_definition: Shows understanding of symptoms/condition and need for care.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize partial insight

mse_library.concise_definition: Partial awareness of condition or need for treatment.
understatement_risks: May underdocument specific areas of impaired insight

mse_library.concise_definition: Limited awareness or acknowledgment of illness/impairment.
understatement_risks: May undercall anosognosia if clinician avoids stronger wording

mse_library.concise_definition: Decision-making appears adequate in observed context.
mse_library.typical_chart_use: Low precision unless based on examples or scenario testing.
understatement_risks: May underrecognize impulsive or unsafe behavior history

mse_library.concise_definition: Decision-making appears unsafe or poorly reasoned.
overstatement_risks: Can be subjective and paternalistic without examples
understatement_risks: May understate acute danger if examples are omitted

risk_language_library.meaning: Passive death-related thought without explicit self-killing plan or desire to act.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Different from active suicidal ideation.
May still be clinically serious and should not be dismissed.
common_misuse_risks: Softened to 'no SI' in some notes.
Strengthened to active SI without clarification.

risk_language_library.meaning: Passive thoughts about death or not waking up without stated intent or plan.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Different from active SI and from nonsuicidal self-harm.
common_misuse_risks: Used inconsistently; some clinicians apply 'SI' only when active.

risk_language_library.meaning: Thoughts of killing oneself.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Needs assessment of frequency, method, plan, intent, and access to means.
common_misuse_risks: Collapsed into a yes/no field without severity context.

risk_language_library.meaning: Suicidal thoughts with identified method or scenario.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Plan is more specific than ideation alone; intent may still vary.
common_misuse_risks: Confusing plan with intent.
Failing to document access to means.

risk_language_library.meaning: Suicidal ideation with desire or expectation to act.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Intent is distinct from having a plan in mind.
common_misuse_risks: Under-differentiating intent from passive wish to die.

risk_language_library.meaning: Behavior indicating movement toward a suicide attempt.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Distinct from ideation; examples include collecting means or writing a note.
common_misuse_risks: Misfiled under plan only.

risk_language_library.meaning: Recent self-directed potentially injurious behavior with at least some intent to die.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Separate from NSSI, accidental overdose, or preparatory behavior.
common_misuse_risks: Using 'attempt' when intent is unclear.
Using 'gesture' instead of assessing.

risk_language_library.meaning: Self-harm without suicidal intent.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Still clinically important and associated with future risk in some settings.
common_misuse_risks: Softened as 'just self-harm'.
Confused with suicide attempt.

risk_language_library.meaning: Umbrella term for self-harm with intent unspecified.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Not equivalent to NSSI or attempt until intent clarified.
common_misuse_risks: Auto-mapped to suicide attempt or NSSI.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient reports no suicidal ideation when asked.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: This is patient report, not proof of absence of all risk.
common_misuse_risks: Overread as 'no suicide risk'.
Copied forward despite change in status.

risk_language_library.meaning: Clinician did not elicit suicidal ideation during this assessment.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: More bounded than 'no SI' and may be safer when disclosure is uncertain.
common_misuse_risks: Could still sound overconfident if no direct assessment occurred.
risk_language_library.notes: Safer phrasing than categorical absence when source is interview.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient agrees to notify others or not self-harm.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Not equivalent to an effective safety intervention.
common_misuse_risks: False reassurance. Should not substitute for a safety plan.

risk_language_library.meaning: Collaborative safety plan with coping steps, contacts, and emergency actions is documented.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Different from a no-suicide contract.
common_misuse_risks: Treated as proof of low risk rather than mitigation step.

risk_language_library.meaning: Thoughts of killing or seriously harming another person.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Separate from generalized anger, irritability, and violent imagery.
common_misuse_risks: Used when only anger is documented.

risk_language_library.meaning: Thoughts of harming others, sometimes less specific than HI.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: May include assaultive fantasies not amounting to homicidal ideation.
common_misuse_risks: Auto-escalated to HI without target or lethality context.

risk_language_library.meaning: Verbalized threats toward others.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Threats are behavior/statements, not identical to intent or imminent action.
common_misuse_risks: Recorded as HI without context or target.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient has access to means relevant to self-harm or violence risk.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Access is separate from desire or intent.
common_misuse_risks: Omitted when plan is documented.
Assumed absent when not asked.

risk_language_library.meaning: Hallucinated commands related to harmful behavior.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Need detail on content, distress, belief, and intent to obey.
common_misuse_risks: Strengthened into imminent intent without assessment.
Softened into generic AVH.

risk_language_library.meaning: Suspicion or persecutory thinking; clinical meaning varies widely.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Can refer colloquially to worry or clinically to delusion-like persecutory thought.
common_misuse_risks: Auto-labeled delusion.
Used for justified mistrust or trauma-related vigilance.

risk_language_library.meaning: Thought content judged to represent delusion.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: High-certainty term not interchangeable with odd, rigid, or unlikely beliefs.
common_misuse_risks: Overstatement from sparse evidence.
Patient quote paraphrased too strongly.

risk_language_library.meaning: Observed behavior suggesting attention to nonshared experience.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Observation only; not equivalent to hallucination.
common_misuse_risks: Auto-mapped to hallucinating or psychotic.

risk_language_library.meaning: Motor/emotional activation or distress.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Different from aggression and violence.
common_misuse_risks: Used when patient is simply upset or restless.
Treated as aggression.

risk_language_library.meaning: Behavior directed toward intimidation, threats, or harm.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Different from agitation or irritability.
common_misuse_risks: Overused for loud or oppositional patients.

risk_language_library.meaning: Behavior involving attempted or actual physical assault.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Higher-specificity behavior term than 'aggressive'.
common_misuse_risks: Applied to verbal hostility only.

risk_language_library.meaning: Clinical state appears to be worsening toward crisis or behavioral dyscontrol.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Needs anchor: agitation, threats, psychosis, intoxication, SI, etc.
common_misuse_risks: Vague without specifics.

risk_language_library.meaning: Clinical status worsening from prior baseline or recent functioning.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Useful longitudinally, but vague if baseline is not defined.
common_misuse_risks: Can become boilerplate without examples.
risk_language_library.notes: Common chart term but should be linked to observable changes.

risk_language_library.meaning: No major change or no current acute escalation, depending context.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: In outpatient notes often means unchanged; in inpatient discharge it may imply not imminently unsafe.
common_misuse_risks: False reassurance.
Masks persistent symptoms or risk.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient identifies values, relationships, goals, or beliefs that support staying alive.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Protective factor, not proof of low risk.
common_misuse_risks: Used to override serious risk factors.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient has meaningful supports and/or agrees to contact them.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Support availability is different from support effectiveness.
common_misuse_risks: Assumed present because family exists.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient expresses future plans, responsibilities, or anticipated events.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Helpful but not sufficient to negate risk.
common_misuse_risks: Used as a blanket reason to call risk low.

risk_language_library.meaning: Patient demonstrates willingness to seek help or use crisis resources.
distinctions_from_similar_terms: Protective only if realistic and accessible.
common_misuse_risks: Treated as proof of safety despite severe acute risk.

patient_language_equivalent: I lose my train of thought

common_provider_wording: too much detail but gets back to point

patient_language_equivalent: I forget what I was saying

common_provider_wording: thinks people are out to get him

patient_language_equivalent: I know they implanted a chip

common_provider_wording: thinks TV/social media is about them

patient_language_equivalent: I hear people talking when nobody is there

common_provider_wording: voices tell pt to hurt self
patient_language_equivalent: the voices tell me what to do

aliases.notes: Observation should not auto-map to hallucination.

patient_language_equivalent: I feel like I'm outside my body
I barely slept but have energy

common_provider_wording: feels like event is happening again

patient_language_equivalent: I can't relax because I'm watching everything
I zone out and don't feel real
I've been working on staying sober
I wish I wouldn't wake up
I've thought about how I'd do it
I think I would do it
I hurt myself to feel better, not to die
I don't want to talk about that
I don't think anything is wrong
I did something risky without thinking
my memory isn't what it used to be but I'm still independent

aliases.notes: 'Sundowning' is common chart language but less precise than delirium descriptors.

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Blames the patient and hides barriers, preference, access, side effects, or informed refusal.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: not taking medication as prescribed; declined; stopped because of side effects; unable to obtain medication

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Stigmatizing and inconsistent with person-first addiction language.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: person with substance use disorder; person who uses drugs

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Derogatory identity label unless explicitly self-identified and context demands quote.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: person with OUD/SUD; person in recovery; person who uses drugs

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Moralizes toxicology results.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: negative/positive toxicology screen

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Can sound criminalizing or moralizing.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: died by suicide

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Minimizes potentially lethal or clinically significant self-harm.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: suicide attempt; self-injurious behavior; NSSI; preparatory behavior, depending facts

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: May create false reassurance and is not an effective substitute for safety planning.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: safety plan completed / discussed crisis resources

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Often overread as 'no suicide risk' and may be copied forward as categorical absence.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: denies suicidal ideation during interview; no suicidal ideation elicited today

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Too vague in psychiatry; may hide persistent symptoms or residual risk.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: specify what is stable: no acute SI, no recent behavioral escalation, mood unchanged from last visit

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Useful shorthand but often too vague without anchor.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: worsening hallucinations; increased agitation; declining sleep and organization; more withdrawn from baseline

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Judgmental and often obscures the observed behavior or function.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: frequently requests discharge after limit-setting; threatened self-harm after conflict; repeated splitting behavior observed

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Pejorative and bias-prone when used without context.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: requests specific medication; reports uncontrolled pain/anxiety and asks for benzodiazepine/opioid

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Often shorthand for frustration with the patient rather than a supported diagnosis.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: describe the behavior or, if clinically supported, use borderline personality disorder

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Person-first language is generally safer, especially in patient-visible notes.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: person with schizophrenia

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Outdated, gendered, and nonspecific.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: acutely anxious; crying; panicked; emotionally overwhelmed

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Outdated and replaced in current usage.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: intellectual disability / intellectual developmental disorder

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Legacy subtype taxonomy may still appear in history, but current umbrella is autism spectrum disorder.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: autism spectrum disorder; retain patient self-identified wording in quotes/context if relevant

terms_to_avoid.term: substance abuse / dependence (when modern SUD wording is intended)
terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Legacy terminology can reduce precision and perpetuate stigma.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: substance use disorder; specify substance and severity if known

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: May mean anxiety, vigilance, trauma response, or delusional persecutory content.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: suspicious; worried others may harm him; reports persecutory beliefs; hypervigilant

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Sounds more certain than source often supports if no hallucinations/delusions/disorganization are documented.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: possible psychotic symptoms; reports auditory hallucinations; appears disorganized

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: High-certainty label that should not be inferred from odd or unlikely beliefs alone.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: reports unusual belief; possible delusional content; fixed persecutory belief if evidence is clear

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Can imply volitional opposition when access, understanding, fear, or side effects explain the decision.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: declined medication after discussion; not interested at this time; stopped due to side effects; unable to obtain

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Can signal disbelief or bias when used asymmetrically.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: states; reports; says

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: May stigmatize and obscures why history quality is limited.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: history limited by memory impairment / disorganization / intoxication / guardedness / language barrier

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Common but vague unless baseline is defined.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: back to reported outpatient baseline per family; similar to prior clinic note; no acute change from yesterday

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Common chart term but imprecise and may miss delirium framing.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: late-day worsening confusion/agitation; fluctuating attention concerning for delirium

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: May falsely imply zero medical contribution to psychiatric symptoms.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: no acute medical issue identified on ED evaluation; medically stable for psychiatric transfer per ED team

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Historically used but can be stigmatizing and underspecified.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: describe the observed behavior or belief precisely

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Conflates distress/restlessness with threatening or assaultive behavior.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: agitated; restless; loud; pacing; threatening; assaultive, whichever matches facts

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Forensic term with major implications; easy to misuse when frustrated by inconsistency.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: inconsistencies noted; secondary gain concerns raised; behavior not fully explained by observed symptoms

terms_to_avoid.why_risky: Psychodynamic shorthand that can sound dismissive in patient-visible notes.
terms_to_avoid.safer_alternative: does not agree that condition is present; attributes symptoms to stress; declines recommended treatment

product_design_recommendations.purpose: Resolve psych abbreviations conservatively, preserve ambiguity, and prevent unsafe auto-expansion.
Normalize psychiatric terminology across documentation, patient language, and chart shorthand while preserving clinical meaning.
Map narrative note language to structured MSE domains without overinterpreting or silently upgrading findings.
Detect and review suicide, self-harm, violence, psychosis-risk, agitation, and crisis language; protect against softening/strengthening.
Flag terms that are variable, local, vague, setting-dependent, or commonly misused.
Detect outdated or stigmatizing language and suggest safer alternatives or internal normalization.
Apply inpatient/ED/outpatient/telehealth modifiers for abbreviation interpretation, risk wording, and note-style defaults.

setting_modifier_examples.risk_threshold_modifier: requires direct symptom confirmation before expanding to hallucinations
setting_modifier_examples.notes: Common on acute units; do not equate with AVH.

setting_modifier_examples.preferred_interpretation: unchanged from recent baseline unless clarified otherwise
setting_modifier_examples.risk_threshold_modifier: never infer low risk from 'stable' alone
setting_modifier_examples.notes: Outpatient 'stable' often means no major change, not asymptomatic.

setting_modifier_examples.preferred_interpretation: not currently requiring inpatient containment by treating team's judgment
setting_modifier_examples.risk_threshold_modifier: still preserve residual symptoms and risk factors

setting_modifier_examples.preferred_interpretation: patient location/contact confirmed for emergency planning
setting_modifier_examples.notes: Telehealth-specific risk documentation needs location and emergency contacts.

setting_modifier_examples.risk_threshold_modifier: must branch to passive/active/plan/intent/preparation with negation support

json_ready_schema.type: lexicon | abbreviation | mse | risk_language | alias | avoid_term
